summary: Profile the 5 OpenAI Build Week judges and score how a DFIR/security project lands

profiles.judge: Thibault Sottiaux ("Tibo"), OpenAI Head of Product & Platform — X: @thsottiaux, GitHub: tibo-openai
profiles.background: VERIFIED: Belgian engineer; BSc + MSc in Computational and Applied Mathematics from UCLouvain (https://iq.wiki/wiki/thibault-sottiaux). Prior career at Google/Google DeepMind: SWE → Staff SWE, ML Workflow Lead, and Gemini Human Data Lead building AI/ML workflow infrastructure (https://iq.wiki/wiki/thibault-sottiaux; VivaTech press release describes him as previously at Google DeepMind on AI/ML workflow infra: https://vivatech.com/media/press-releases/breaking-news-peter-steinberger-creator-of-openclaw-and-thibault-sottiaux-openai-two-ai-experts-for-an-exceptional-session-at-vivatech). Joined OpenAI in 2025 as Codex engineering lead (his X bio notes joining Aug 2025). In 2026 promoted to head of core product & platform ("Head of Product & Platform") under president Greg Brockman, overseeing ChatGPT, Codex, and the API platform serving 900M+ users, driving the ChatGPT+Codex "super app" / "personal AGI" merger ahead of a potential IPO (https://fortune.com/2026/06/22/architectural-behind-openais-super-app-says/; VivaTech PR). He owns the product org that now includes the OpenClaw teams — his own post: "Much of the teams building ChatGPT, Codex and OpenClaw will be there" (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2074195169990357398). Judged the Feb 5, 2026 OpenAI Codex hackathon at OpenAI HQ (jury: Greg Brockman, Sonya Huang, Sottiaux, Lenny Rachitsky, Peter Steinberger), won by Rippletide's continuously-running multi-agent system with an explicit "decision layer" that scores/verifies agent outputs (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer). Confirmed judge for OpenAI Build Week alongside Kath Korevec, Tara Seshan, Leah Belsky, Peter Steinberger (https://openai.devpost.com/). Runs "100 Days of Codex," personally rewarding one person per day "who does impressive or incredibly useful work with Codex" with 10x limits (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2063748242681307611).
public_taste: Co-authored OpenAI's 'A Practical Approach to Verifying Code at Scale' (Dec 2025) — treats automated code review as an output monitor in a defense-in-depth safety strategy; key line: defenses 'often fail not because they are technically wrong, but because they are so impractical that the user chooses not to use them' — he deliberately prioritized precision over recall. https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/

Verification as daily lived practice: 'Every single PR that is written in OpenAI is always reviewed by Codex, and it acts as this safety net... it's hard to think about the world where we wouldn't have that safety net anymore, given how many critical flaws it catches every day' (SED podcast transcript, Jan 29 2026). http://softwareengineeringdaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/SED1898-OpenAI.txt

Anti-scaffolding minimalism: episode literally titled 'Scaffolding is coping not scaling' — 'simplicity just often leads to better scaling over time'; excessive harness creates 'capability overhang' that 'prevents [the model] from expressing its true capability' (Dev Interrupted, Jan 27 2026). https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy

Agent-first product philosophy: build a capable general agent first, then find where it creates value; values Codex use beyond code generation — 'pull request reviews, log analysis, performance testing... the bottlenecks'. https://linearb.io/blog/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy

Super-app / personal-AGI vision; explicitly rejects a consumer/enterprise divide — same tech wins by 'connecting the right context, connecting the right tools, and then taking actions in a way that minimizes risk' (Fortune, Jun 22 2026). https://fortune.com/2026/06/22/architectural-behind-openais-super-app-says/

Public accountability/transparency instinct: 'We promised unprecedented transparency for Codex and to take the reports of degradation seriously... Here is our report' (X, ~Nov 2025). https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/1984465716888944712

Rewards deep, ambitious Codex usage: '100 Days of Codex' — 10x limits for people doing 'impressive or incredibly useful work with Codex'; showcased picks include autonomous game-building, agentic video-editing harnesses, multilingual dictionary pipelines (X + digg coverage). https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2063748242681307611 and https://digg.com/ai/kbk480zy

Design/delight sensibility: publicly gushes over Codex designer Ed Bayes's UI ('Sometimes I get stuck here in awe for how pretty @edbayes made this'), and 'Always be shipping. This one is fun and delightful'; wants agents to feel 'bubbly... super collaborative... personal'. https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/1965914071582326789 ; https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2032224054963552541 ; https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy

Engineering-culture signals via Pragmatic Engineer: chose Rust for Codex CLI partly for correctness and reviewable dependencies (explicit wariness of npm supply chain); team runs 4-8 parallel agents doing features, code review, and security audits; celebrated Codex autonomously debugging its own systems 'connected to logs, SSH'd into research dev boxes'. https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/how-codex-is-built

Co-judged the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon won by Rippletide's verification/'decision layer' system ('The hard part is not generating outputs. The hard part is turning them into outcomes') — NOTE: no individual quote from Sottiaux praising it was found; the Rippletide post credits the jury collectively. https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer
Build Week framing (he is speaking at the July 13 opening session): 'Builders of the world. This is your time.' https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2075322213834875026
profiles.security_affinity: STRONG on trust/verification/safety-engineering; ZERO documented interest in DFIR/forensics specifically. Positive evidence: (1) he is a listed co-author of OpenAI's alignment post on verifying AI-generated code at scale — defense-in-depth, output monitoring, precision-first design (https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/); (2) hard-line sandboxing stance: 'It doesn't matter how convenient it is to run outside of a sandbox. Ultimately, you are giving control to a very capable and intelligent entity to do whatever it would want to do to your machine using your own credentials' and 'We do really feel the responsibility... to make sure that there are no unintended negative consequences' (SED transcript, http://softwareengineeringdaily.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/SED1898-OpenAI.txt); he even accepts that sandboxing 'hurts us in terms of general adoption' (https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/how-codex-is-built); (3) supply-chain wariness (Rust over npm) and internal agents running 'security audits' (same Pragmatic Engineer source); (4) enterprise framing of 'taking actions in a way that minimizes risk' (https://fortune.com/2026/06/22/architectural-behind-openais-super-app-says/); (5) context: the OpenClaw security crisis (800+ malicious skills, exposed instances, Feb-May 2026 — https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/openclaw-ai-supply-chain-risk/, https://www.reco.ai/blog/openclaw-the-ai-agent-security-crisis-unfolding-right-now) unfolded inside his extended portfolio, since he says his org includes 'teams building ChatGPT, Codex and OpenClaw' (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2074195169990357398), and OpenAI's Aardvark security agent was folded into 'Codex Security' in March 2026 (https://openai.com/index/introducing-aardvark/) — so agent trust/security is a live, board-level topic for him in July 2026. EXPLICIT GAPS: I found no public post, talk, or comment by Sottiaux about DFIR, digital forensics, incident response, courtroom/legal evidence, or compliance tooling (targeted searches returned nothing); and no public statement by him specifically addressing the OpenClaw security incidents. His security interest is agent-safety-flavored (sandboxes, verification, monitors), not investigator/practitioner-flavored.
what_impresses_them: Verification and 'decision layers' that turn agent outputs into trusted outcomes — his own alignment publication (https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/) and the Rippletide win he co-judged (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer) are both exactly this pattern

Deep, genuine Codex proficiency — long-running autonomous sessions, parallel agents, agent-managed workflows; he personally rewards 'impressive or incredibly useful work with Codex' daily (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2063748242681307611); note Technological Implementation is also the hackathon tiebreaker (https://openai.devpost.com/)
Continuously-running background agent systems that accumulate work over time, vs one-shot chatbots (Rippletide post; Dev Interrupted)
Simple, scalable harnesses — 'delightfully simple' primitives that get better as models improve, minimal scaffolding ('it's a for loop and then a bunch of tool calls', SED transcript)

Agents doing investigative, non-codegen work: log analysis, PR review, self-debugging via logs/SSH — things he cites approvingly (https://linearb.io/blog/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy; https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/how-codex-is-built)
Safety done practically: sandboxed-by-default, defense in depth, high-precision monitors people actually use (alignment post; SED)
Polished, delightful, personal product design — the Ed Bayes school of UI (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/1965914071582326789)

Small ambitious teams shipping fast: 'Small teams that have a lot of energy and ambition are able to achieve so much more' (SED transcript); 'Always be shipping' (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2032224054963552541)
what_bores_or_alienates_them: Heavy bespoke scaffolding presented as the achievement — 'scaffolding is coping not scaling'; harnesses that 'constrain [the model] in a way that prevents it from expressing its true capability' (https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy). INFERENCE: a demo framed as '300 wrapped forensic CLI tools' risks reading as exactly this; frame the tools as context/actuators and the model's autonomous reasoning + verification loop as the star

Impractical or friction-heavy 'safety theater' — 'defenses often fail... because they are so impractical that the user chooses not to use them' (https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/); a courtroom-grade tool that feels bureaucratic rather than usable would trip this wire

Thin prompt-loops and chatbots with no evaluation logic — the anti-pattern the Rippletide post contrasts against the winning entry (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer)
Over-opinionated frameworks that restrict flexibility: 'We try to be very mindful when we introduce more opinionated frameworks... that could restrict that flexibility' (SED transcript)
Demos that run agents unsandboxed / with reckless permissions — directly against his stated convictions (SED transcript sandbox quote)

INFERENCE (no direct quote): narrow-vertical pitches that don't connect to a bigger 'agents doing real work for everyone' story — everything he promotes (super app, Codex-is-for-everyone, personal AGI) pushes toward broad applicability (https://fortune.com/2026/06/22/architectural-behind-openais-super-app-says/; https://forum.openai.com/public/events/codex-is-for-everyone-why-codex-matters-beyond-code-fa40puy7wi)
Unpolished, joyless UX — Design is a full quarter of the score and he demonstrably cares about delight (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/1965914071582326789)
profiles.dfir_score_rationale: The project's CORE THESIS — an autonomous agent whose findings are machine-checkable and self-verified — is the single most documented obsession in Sottiaux's public record: he co-authored OpenAI's verification-at-scale post (https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/), calls Codex code review a 'safety net' catching 'critical flaws every day' (SED transcript), and sat on the jury that gave the Feb 2026 hackathon to Rippletide's verification/decision-layer system (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer). Agent trust is also commercially live for him in July 2026 given the OpenClaw security crisis inside his extended org (https://x.com/thsottiaux/status/2074195169990357398; https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/openclaw-ai-supply-chain-risk/), and he has publicly praised investigation-shaped agent work (log analysis, Codex self-debugging via logs/SSH — https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/how-codex-is-built). He also explicitly denies any consumer/enterprise divide (Fortune), so 'professional B2B' is not disqualifying. That puts a well-executed entry meaningfully above the median consumer-app or to-do-list-agent competition on Quality of Idea and on the tiebreak criterion (Codex proficiency — which he personally celebrates via 100 Days of Codex). WHY NOT 9: (1) zero evidence of personal interest in DFIR/forensics/courtroom evidence — the domain itself buys nothing with him; (2) real 'scaffolding is coping' risk: a demo centered on hundreds of wrapped forensic tools reads as the constraining-harness anti-pattern he preaches against — the pitch must center the autonomous verification loop and machine-checkable citation primitive ('delightfully simple'), not the tool inventory; (3) judge legibility: he cannot assess forensic correctness in a 3-minute review, and Design (25%) favors polished consumer experiences — the report/citation UX must be genuinely beautiful; (4) Potential Impact may read narrower to a man steering a 900M-user super app than an education or productivity entry, unless framed as 'the trust layer any agent needs before it can do high-stakes work' — the exact framing that won Rippletide the last hackathon he judged. Net: 7/10 with upside to 8-9 if pitched as a self-verifying autonomous investigator (trust/verification-first, forensics as the proving ground) and downside to 4-5 if pitched as a security tool suite.

profiles.judge: Kath (Kathy) Korevec — @simpsoka on X; Codex product lead at OpenAI, listed on the OpenAI Build Week Devpost judge panel as "Member of Product Staff" alongside Thibault Sottiaux, Tara Seshan, Leah Belsky, and Peter Steinberger (verified via https://openai.devpost.com/)
profiles.background: VERIFIED: ~15-year product career, entirely in developer tools/platforms. Path: web design → Google (front-end) → Hot Studio → SAY Media → Disqus → Heroku → GitHub (Senior Director of Product, owned Docs; famous for turning docs "from a static site into an interactive interface" and for 'papercuts' DX-polish pushes — https://github.com/readme/stories/kathy-korevec) → Vercel (Head of Product — https://vercel.com/blog/welcoming-kathy-korevec-to-vercel-our-new-head-of-product) → Google Labs (Director of Product on Jules, Google's async coding agent; gave the 'Proactive Agents' talk at AI Engineer Code Summit, Dec 2025 — https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=v3u8xc0zLec, summary: https://www.startuphub.ai/ai-news/startup-news/2025/proactive-agents-shifting-from-reactive-ai-to-intelligent-collaboration) → OpenAI, product on Codex (her X bio and posts; judge listing at https://openai.devpost.com/). At OpenAI she runs Codex 'papercuts' quality pushes with Thibault Sottiaux (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2048493146833907847), solicits Windows-user pain points ("Tell me the good bad and ugly" — https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2052844313848299732), and promoted the whimsical Codex Pets / hatch-pet skill (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2050303773499908194). Writes the 'Kathy PM' Substack (https://kathykorevec.substack.com) and reads it aloud as the 'Kathy PM Out Loud' podcast (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2008175646862876956). Personal: Alaska-raised, marathon runner, hardware/maker hobbyist (flip-disc displays, beaded-earring design tool, jules-companion iOS app — https://github.com/simpsoka).
public_taste: Agents that CRITIQUE work, not just generate it — her essay 'The crit is the product' (Mar 2026) argues AI design tools are 'compliant interns' and the winning agent is one that 'hears make this more sleek and says, I think the bigger issue is that your hierarchy is unclear'; the crit is 'a structured conversation about whether the work is actually doing its job' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-crit-is-the-product

Accountability as the new scarce resource — 'The end of scarcity' (Jan 2026): with execution cheap, value shifts to 'responsibility over time,' owning systems after novelty fades and 'explaining decisions months later' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-end-of-scarcity

Trust and governed agent behavior — 'Designing for the Long Conversation' (Jan 2026): 'We are designing relationships now, not features'; 'The biggest failures will be emotional, not technical'; 'working code will no longer be the bar'; warns about 'small trust breaks... moments where you realize you don't actually know why something changed' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/designing-for-the-long-conversation

Context/continuity over destination apps — 'If apps are dead, is context the new moat?': winning products offer 'Longitudinal memory, Cross-tool visibility, Structured, governed action, Trust earned over time' and are 'always on, mostly invisible, and explicitly governed' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/if-apps-are-dead-is-context-the-new

Dogfooding as discipline — 'Build What You Depend On': 'Watching someone else experience pain builds empathy. Feeling the pain yourself builds urgency.' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/build-what-you-depend-on

Whimsy and delight in dev tools — promoted Codex Pets / the hatch-pet skill ('If you want to create and customize your pet, install the hatch-pet skill' — https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2050303773499908194); credited for a 'Codex App Server on Watchy' smartwatch demo (https://www.instagram.com/reel/DXLLwQ1jkez/)

Maker/hardware creativity — built 'an agent for researching, coding, and running generative art animations for 16-segment displays' (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2022795109310537859) and a large flip-disc panel project (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/1815850349846225035); GitHub is full of firmware-to-frontend hobby projects (https://github.com/simpsoka)

DX polish / papercuts culture — 'You know u love a good papercuts push. Go tell Tibo what we should fix.' (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2048493146833907847); at GitHub she was known for papercuts and docs-as-product ('just as you wouldn't ship untested code, shipping code without documentation is not optional' — Ship It! #17, https://changelog.com/person/simpsoka)

Durability beyond the demo — on Jules: users 'vibe-code their way to a prototype, then hit the vibe code limit... bring it to Jules, more durable, debug it, add services, and turn it into something real' — https://x.com/simpsoka/status/1966876712526115226

Interfaces that meet users where they are — 'Prime example of how AI is making it so that we only use the interface we *want* to use' (building iOS apps without Xcode) — https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2024211999664853033

Codex's breadth as developer reality — 'developers do a lot more than just write code... It's because we built it for developers' (quoted at https://www.manton.org/2026/06/01/kath-korevec-who-works-at.html)

Proactive agents that reduce mental load — 'We want Jules to do the dishes without being asked'; her 'attentive sous chef' level = an agent 'detecting missing tests, unused dependencies, or unsafe patterns and proactively suggesting or implementing fixes' — https://www.startuphub.ai/ai-news/startup-news/2025/proactive-agents-shifting-from-reactive-ai-to-intelligent-collaboration
profiles.security_affinity: HONEST ANSWER: essentially no direct security/DFIR/infosec footprint found. Searches of her X posts, Substack, talks and podcasts surfaced zero posts about security incidents, forensics, incident response, compliance, or the OpenClaw early-2026 security meltdown (the OpenClaw security coverage — Snyk, CrowdStrike, Cisco, Giskard — never mentions her; no @simpsoka commentary found). Her X *likes* could not be retrieved (X blocks unauthenticated scraping; mirror sites returned 403), so absence of like-data is a research gap, not proof of disinterest. CLOSEST ADJACENT SIGNALS (verified): (1) her Proactive Agents talk praises agents that detect 'unsafe patterns' proactively (https://www.startuphub.ai/ai-news/startup-news/2025/proactive-agents-shifting-from-reactive-ai-to-intelligent-collaboration); (2) her 'context moat' essay explicitly values 'Structured, governed action' and 'Trust earned over time' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/if-apps-are-dead-is-context-the-new); (3) 'Designing for the Long Conversation' is heavily about trust, behavioral consistency, and knowing 'why something changed' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/designing-for-the-long-conversation); (4) 'The crit is the product' is philosophically pro-verification: agents should challenge output, not rubber-stamp it (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-crit-is-the-product). INFERENCE: she cares about trust, verification, and accountability as PRODUCT and RELATIONSHIP qualities, not as a security discipline. She has never worked in security; her framing vocabulary is 'trust,' 'crit,' 'governed,' 'responsibility' — not 'chain of custody,' 'forensically sound,' or 'courtroom-grade.' Note also: the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon (judged by Sottiaux and Steinberger, both on THIS panel too, plus Brockman/Huang/Rachitsky — https://x.com/OpenAIDevs/status/2019902197199434190) was won by Rippletide's decision-layer/evaluation system (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer); no judge quotes praising it were published, but the panel-overlap precedent shows this judging pool rewards verification-layer systems over flashy chatbots. Korevec was not a judge at that event.
what_impresses_them: An agent that audits and challenges its own output — a built-in 'crit loop' is literally the product thesis of her most recent essay ('design doesn't benefit from blind agreement. It benefits from tension and constraint' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-crit-is-the-product); a self-verifying investigator that says 'this finding fails cite-check, downgrading confidence' is her crit-mode made real

Accountability artifacts — machine-checkable citations map directly onto her claim that the new scarce value is 'responsibility over time' and 'explaining decisions months later' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-end-of-scarcity)

Deep, native Codex usage — she is the Codex product person and runs papercuts pushes; sophisticated use of Codex primitives (skills, MCP tools, app server, long-running agents, multi-surface) hits the tie-breaking 'Technological Implementation' criterion she is best positioned to evaluate (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2048493146833907847)

Legible design that tells a story — ex-GitHub-docs leader; onboarding, docs, and information design are her home turf ('If I can make it easier for developers to onboard...they'll use tools more' — https://github.com/readme/stories/kathy-korevec); a beautiful evidence-timeline UI beats a wall of terminal output

Durable systems beyond the vibe-code demo — 'turn it into something real' (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/1966876712526115226) and 'working code will no longer be the bar' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/designing-for-the-long-conversation)

Proactive, mental-load-reducing autonomy — agents that 'do the dishes without being asked' and know when to interrupt vs stay silent (https://www.startuphub.ai/ai-news/startup-news/2025/proactive-agents-shifting-from-reactive-ai-to-intelligent-collaboration)
A touch of delight/whimsy and maker-spirit — Codex pets, Watchy, flip-disc art (https://x.com/simpsoka/status/2050303773499908194, https://www.instagram.com/reel/DXLLwQ1jkez/)

Evidence of real dogfooding — showing you used the tool on a real case/incident, with the friction you fixed because you personally felt it (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/build-what-you-depend-on)
what_bores_or_alienates_them: Domain jargon she can't evaluate or dogfood — 'courtroom-grade,' 'chain of custody,' EWF/Volatility/plaso name-dropping; her dogfooding philosophy means she judges tools partly by imagining herself using them, and she will never run a forensic investigation ('Feeling the pain yourself builds urgency' — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/build-what-you-depend-on). INFERENCE from her stated philosophy, not a documented complaint about security tools

Compliant-intern demos — agents that just generate impressive-looking output with no tension, critique, or self-challenge ('compliant interns' is her pejorative — https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-crit-is-the-product)

'Destination' apps that demand context-switching — 'Asking someone to use your app today feels like inviting them to cook a fancy dinner in someone else's house' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/if-apps-are-dead-is-context-the-new); a monolithic forensic console reads as a destination app unless it meets analysts inside their existing workflow
Working-code-only pitches with no trust/maintainability story — 'working code will no longer be the bar' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/designing-for-the-long-conversation)
Ugly or undocumented tools — docs are 'not optional' to her (https://changelog.com/person/simpsoka); a hackathon entry with no onboarding path or docs will register as unfinished

Fear-based security marketing — INFERENCE: no evidence she engages with threat-scare framing at all; her entire public voice is optimistic builder-energy (pets, papercuts, maker projects), so 'attackers will get you' framing is more likely to bounce off than to resonate. No verified statement of hers disparaging security tools exists — this is absence of affinity, not documented aversion
profiles.dfir_score_rationale: SCORE 6.5/10 — above the median entry, with high variance depending entirely on framing. WHY NOT LOWER: the contestant's core mechanism — a self-verifying agent with machine-checkable evidence citations — is an almost uncanny match for Korevec's 2026 writing: 'The crit is the product' (agents must challenge their own work, not rubber-stamp it), 'The end of scarcity' ('responsibility over time' and 'explaining decisions months later' as the new scarce value), and 'Designing for the Long Conversation' ('working code will no longer be the bar'; trust breaks when 'you don't actually know why something changed'). A DFIR investigator that refuses to assert an unverifiable finding IS her crit-mode agent, applied to a domain where hallucination is disqualifying. Precedent also helps: the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon — judged by Sottiaux and Steinberger, both on this July panel with her — was won by Rippletide's verification/decision-layer system, not a consumer toy (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer). And her 20-year career is professional B2B developer tooling, not consumer apps — she will not penalize 'professional tool' per se. Deep Codex-primitive usage (skills, MCP, long-running agents) plays directly to the tie-breaking Technological Implementation criterion she is uniquely qualified to judge. WHY NOT HIGHER: (1) Zero verified security/DFIR affinity — no tweets, essays, or talks touching infosec, IR, or the OpenClaw incidents; forensics is a domain she cannot dogfood, and her stated method for judging tools is feeling the pain herself. (2) Her demonstrated public delight skews to whimsy, polish, and maker-joy (Codex pets, Watchy smartwatch server, flip-disc art, papercuts pushes) — a dense forensic console risks scoring low on her Design bar unless the evidence-citation UX is genuinely beautiful and legible to a non-forensics viewer. (3) 'Courtroom-grade' legal framing is outside her vocabulary; her words are 'trust,' 'crit,' 'governed action,' 'accountability.' TACTICAL IMPLICATION: pitched as 'a security product for DFIR professionals,' it lands ~5 with her; pitched as 'an autonomous agent that proves every claim it makes — the crit loop and accountability layer agents have been missing, demonstrated on the hardest truth-critical domain there is,' with a clean visual evidence-graph UI, real dogfooding narrative, docs, and maybe one moment of wit, it lands 7.5-8 with her — likely stronger than the median consumer/productivity entry precisely because most rival entries will be the 'compliant interns' she has publicly criticized. Enter it in Developer Tools or Work & Productivity, lead with verification-not-forensics, and let the DFIR case study be the proof, not the pitch.

A verification-layer project (Rippletide) also just won the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon under a panel sharing two judges with this one — the theme is not 'too security-coded' for this ecosystem. AGAINST: she has zero demonstrated personal interest in security/DFIR; her current center of gravity is mainstream knowledge-work delegation for 'everyday users'; and she has a high, explicit bar for design legibility and product completeness — a terminal-and-log-tables forensics demo will read as a Retool-style expert power-tool and lose her. Versus the likely competition (consumer apps, productivity agents, dev tools, ed-tech), the DFIR entry can beat the field with her on Quality of Idea and Potential Impact — but only if the demo leads with the polished, clickable final artifact (a report where every claim links to verifiable evidence, legible to a non-forensics juror/judge persona), frames it as 'delegate an investigation, get a defensible artifact', and shows deep Codex usage (the tie-breaker criterion). Pitched as raw DFIR tooling: ~4/10 with her. Pitched as the trust layer for agent-delegated professional work with a walk-in-able UI: ~7-8/10. Net: 6.5.

profiles.dfir_score_rationale: Verified basis: Belsky has zero demonstrable engagement with security/DFIR topics, and every project she has publicly praised or funded is a human-legible education/access/impact story (Futures grants, Estonia grading agent, Study Mode). Against likely Build Week competition — consumer life apps, productivity agents, dev tools, and ed-tech that speaks her native language — a courtroom-grade forensic investigator pitched in DFIR vocabulary lands around 4/10 with her: strong on the Technological Implementation criterion (deep agentic Codex usage, self-verification loops — and ties break on this), but weak on her felt sense of Potential Impact and Quality of Idea because the user, stakes, and market are outside her world, and Design praise requires her to follow a demo she has no domain intuition for. Two honest caveats cut both ways. Upside: the single best proxy we have — the Estonia hackathon winner she personally spotlighted — is structurally identical to this project (agent automates repetitive verification drudgery, quantified hours saved, human expert retains final authority), and her Yale Law background makes 'machine-checkable evidence citations that survive courtroom scrutiny' unusually legible to her; pitched as 'this gives every under-resourced organization access to elite investigator expertise, saves an examiner 300 hours per case, and never asserts a finding it can't cite — the analyst stays in charge,' the same project plausibly rises to 6-6.5/10. Downside: pitched as raw forensics tooling with hex dumps and artifact jargon, it drops to 3. Also note she was NOT on the Feb 2026 Codex panel that rewarded Rippletide's verification layer (that was Brockman/Huang/Sottiaux/Rachitsky/Steinberger — https://x.com/OpenAIDevs/status/2019902197199434190), so that precedent cannot be assumed to transfer to her. Net: 4/10 as-conceived for this judge; the fix is not changing the project but changing the story — hours saved, expert amplified, trust made checkable, access democratized. profiles.judge: Peter Steinberger (@steipete) — Member of Technical Staff, OpenAI ("Clawfather"); confirmed judge on the OpenAI Build Week Devpost panel (verified at https://openai.devpost.com/ alongside Thibault Sottiaux, Kath Korevec, Tara Seshan, Leah Belsky)
profiles.background: VERIFIED: Austrian developer, Vienna University of Technology. profiles.security_affinity: Unusually high salience, but complicated — he is a security-incident SURVIVOR, not a security-industry fan. VERIFIED CRISIS: OpenClaw's Jan-Feb 2026 crisis included RCE CVE-2026-25253, the ClawHavoc campaign (hundreds to 1,184 malicious ClawHub skills, AMOS stealer, ~$2.3M crypto stolen per some reports), and 21k-30k+ internet-exposed instances (https://conscia.com/blog/the-openclaw-security-crisis/; https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/openclaw-ai-supply-chain-risk/; https://www.kaspersky.com/blog/openclaw-vulnerabilities-exposed/55263/; https://www.theregister.com/2026/02/02/openclaw_security_issues/). VERIFIED RESPONSE: he personally co-announced the VirusTotal partnership (Feb 7, 2026) — LLM-powered Code Insight scanning of every ClawHub skill, auto-blocking malicious ones — plus a published threat model, public security roadmap, full-codebase audit, and hiring Jamieson O'Reilly as lead security advisor (https://openclaw.ai/blog/virustotal-partnership; https://thehackernews.com/2026/02/openclaw-integrates-virustotal-scanning.html). VERIFIED ATTITUDE (Lex Fridman #491, https://lexfridman.com/peter-steinberger-transcript/): pragmatic and mildly defensive — 'people tried to prompt inject it, and my bot would laugh at them'; 'You can also now have a sandbox. You can have an allow list'; 'don't use cheap models'; 'If you make sure you are the only person talking to it, the risk profile is much smaller.' VERIFIED VERIFICATION/HALLUCINATION CONCERN: 'AI is incredibly powerful, but it's not always right... it's very easy that it just hallucinates something'; 'I actually do look at the code because I don't trust people. Like, there could always be something malicious in it'; frustrated that 'I literally had to argue with people that told me, Yeah, but my agent said this and this.' VERIFIED SORENESS: he frames much security criticism as dunking — called a critical thread 'a masterclass in the amount of vitriol people face when working on open source' (https://x.com/steipete/status/2071972239734616146) and pushed back on crisis narratives: 'The hype died down. We improved quality... This is our strongest week so far' (https://x.com/steipete/status/2068961217524490739). NO EVIDENCE FOUND of any specific interest in DFIR/incident-response/forensics as a discipline, courtroom evidence standards, or compliance tooling — though his own hobby tools (wacrawl encrypted-backup extraction, chat-archive crawlers) are functionally forensic artifact extraction (https://github.com/steipete).
what_impresses_them: A live, working demo that runs during judging — he ships in hours and measures others by the same clock ('built in one hour', 9k stars in 24h is his own origin story; https://n9o.xyz/posts/202602-steipete-openclaw-openai/)

Heavy, visible Codex leverage — the tie-break criterion is his home turf; he tweets Codex maintenance loops (https://x.com/steipete/status/2064998499780084154) and Romain Huet cites him as the exemplar Codex builder (https://x.com/romainhuet/status/2016314931566805182)

Agents that close their own loop: self-testing, self-verifying output rather than trust-me prose (https://steipete.me/posts/2025/shipping-at-inference-speed) — machine-checkable citations map directly onto this

Explicit evaluation/decision layers over ad-hoc prompting — the exact thing the panel he sat on rewarded in Rippletide (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer)
Continuous/background operation that accumulates work over time rather than single-shot chat (same Rippletide source)
Extracting real signal from messy real-world data formats (his own wacrawl/discrawl/birdclaw pattern; https://github.com/steipete)
Personality, wit, and 'human touch' in the product — a demo with character beats a sterile one ('I wanted it to be fun, I wanted it to be weird'; https://lexfridman.com/peter-steinberger-transcript/)
Simplicity of interface — one chat box, one command; 'magic often just... things that are already there but brought together in new ways' (Lex transcript)

Making an expert-only capability usable by non-experts — his stated life mission ('an agent that even my mum can use', https://steipete.me/posts/2026/openclaw); a DFIR tool a non-forensicator can drive would hit this
what_bores_or_alienates_them: Enterprise/compliance framing and corporate self-seriousness — 'they all take themselves too serious... it's hard to compete against someone who's just there to have fun' (https://lexfridman.com/peter-steinberger-transcript/); he also left Europe citing regulation as innovation-inhibiting (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Steinberger_(programmer))

Security FUD and fear-first pitches — he has spent five months being dunked on by security vendors and researchers and reads much of it as 'vitriol' (https://x.com/steipete/status/2071972239734616146); a pitch that opens with 'AI agents can't be trusted' risks sounding like his own critics

Architecture theater: elaborate multi-agent diagrams, subagent orchestration for its own sake, MCP-as-checkbox, spec-driven heavyweight process — all explicitly mocked in 'Just Talk To It' (https://steipete.me/posts/just-talk-to-it)
Slideware over software; anything that cannot be demoed live
Prompt-wall 'agent specs' and autogenerated documentation slop ('an amalgamation of slop... autogenerated soup', same source)
Narrow gatekept expert tools that stay expert-only — his energy goes to democratization ('every time someone made the first pull request is a win for our society', Lex transcript)
Plausible-sounding unverified AI claims — he is annoyed by 'my agent said this and this' credulity (Lex transcript), so any hallucinated citation in the demo would be fatal with him specifically
profiles.dfir_score_rationale: The contestant's fear of being 'too security-coded' is half right but aimed at the wrong thing. VERIFIED tailwinds: (1) Steinberger sat on the jury that gave the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon to Rippletide — a verification/decision-layer system praised for explicit evaluation over ad-hoc heuristics — so 'self-verifying agent' is literally a pattern this judge has already rewarded (rippletide.com blog). (2) Machine-checkable evidence citations attack his most-documented anxiety: hallucination and 'my agent said this' credulity (Lex #491 transcript). (3) He personally built the closest existing analogue — LLM-powered security analysis of untrusted code via the VirusTotal/ClawHub integration he announced himself (openclaw.ai/blog/virustotal-partnership) — and tweets approvingly about 'secure agents you can trust' (x.com/steipete/status/2033641463104323868). (4) His hobby repos (wacrawl encrypted-backup extraction, chat-archive crawlers) show genuine enjoyment of artifact-extraction work. (5) A deep agentic pipeline over real disk images should dominate the Technological Implementation criterion — the tie-breaker — against thin consumer wrappers. VERIFIED headwinds keep it at 7, not 9: 'courtroom-grade' and compliance vocabulary reads as the corporate self-seriousness he mocks; he is sore at the security industry after months of vitriol, so fear-first framing could misfire; DFIR is a narrow professional niche, which hurts Potential Impact next to consumer/education entries and clashes with his 'agent my mum can use' mission; and he rewards personality, live-demo speed, and interface simplicity — areas where forensics tools typically fail. Framing recommendation grounded in his own words: pitch it as 'an agent that proves its work — every claim is a click away from the raw evidence' (his closed-loop ideal), demo it live on a real image, make a non-forensicator drive it, keep the architecture story short, and never say 'compliance' before saying 'it caught its own hallucination.' Caveat: he is one of five judges; scores here predict Steinberger only. Unverifiable: his X likes, and any individual quote from him praising Rippletide (jury membership verified; personal enthusiasm is inference).

verdict.panel_sentiment: This panel's single most documented shared taste is VERIFICATION OF AGENT OUTPUT — not consumer whimsy, and not security. Two of the five judges (Sottiaux, Steinberger) sat on the Feb 2026 Codex hackathon jury that awarded first place to Rippletide's verification/decision-layer system over flashier entries (https://www.rippletide.com/resources/blog/winning-the-openai-codex-hackathon-moving-from-outputs-to-outcomes-the-decision-layer). Sottiaux co-authored OpenAI's post on verifying AI code at scale and calls Codex review a 'safety net' catching 'critical flaws every day' (https://alignment.openai.com/scaling-code-verification/; SED transcript Jan 2026). Korevec's March 2026 essay argues the winning agent is one that critiques its own work rather than acting as a 'compliant intern,' and that the new scarce value is 'responsibility over time' (https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-crit-is-the-product; https://kathykorevec.substack.com/p/the-end-of-scarcity). Seshan's stated agent thesis (Forbes, June 30 2026) is 'define what good looks like... review outputs... get a polished artifact at the end' (https://www.forbes.com/sites/the-prompt/2026/06/30/could-codex-be-the-answer-to-openais-problems/), and Steinberger's core design principle is agents that 'close the loop' by testing their own output, with documented irritation at 'my agent said this' credulity (https://steipete.me/posts/2025/shipping-at-inference-speed; https://lexfridman.com/peter-steinberger-transcript/). At the same time, ZERO of the five judges has any verified personal interest in DFIR, forensics, or courtroom evidence — every analyst searched for it and found nothing — and the panel demonstrably rewards polish, delight, human stories, and democratization (Sottiaux on Ed Bayes's UI; Korevec's Codex Pets; Belsky's Estonia grading winner with its 378→100 hours-saved story, https://edunewsletter.openai.com/p/the-future-starts-in-this-room; Steinberger's 'agent even my mum can use'). Net read: the contestant's VERIFICATION MECHANISM is the best-aligned idea any entrant could bring to this specific panel; his FORENSICS VOCABULARY is the only part that is a liability. The fear is half right but aimed at the wrong layer.

security_coded_risk.concept: A — AIRTIGHT (self-verifying autonomous forensic investigator, Work & Productivity)
security_coded_risk.effect: HELPS if framed as verification, HURTS if framed as security. Rationale: the core mechanism (machine-checkable citations, adversarial verifier, coverage auditor) is a direct hit on four judges' documented obsessions — Sottiaux's verification-at-scale publication and the Rippletide precedent he co-judged; Korevec's crit-loop and accountability essays; Seshan's review-outputs/polished-artifact thesis plus her Radar/Treasury/Watershed audit-trail career; Steinberger's closed-loop principle and hallucination anxiety. Analyst scores as-conceived: Sottiaux 7 (upside 8-9 pitched verification-first, downside 4-5 as 'security tool suite'), Korevec 6.5 (~5 as 'security product for DFIR pros', 7.5-8 as 'agent that proves every claim'), Seshan 6.5 (~4 as raw DFIR tooling, 7-8 as trust layer for delegated work), Steinberger 7, Belsky 4 (rises to 6-6.5 with hours-saved/access framing, drops to 3 with hex dumps). The 'security-coded' penalty is real but specific: DFIR jargon Belsky/Korevec cannot evaluate, 'courtroom-grade/compliance' vocabulary that trips Steinberger's anti-corporate-seriousness (he calls security criticism 'vitriol' after five months of OpenClaw dunking — https://x.com/steipete/status/2071972239734616146), and a 300-tool inventory that trips Sottiaux's 'scaffolding is coping' wire (https://linearb.io/dev-interrupted/podcast/openai-codex-thibault-sottiaux-agentic-autonomy). All three penalties are framing-removable; the verification upside is structural.

security_coded_risk.concept: B — CaseForge (AI DFIR instructor with planted ground truth, Education)
security_coded_risk.effect: NEUTRAL-to-mildly-helps on the 'security-coded' axis but strategically weaker overall. Rationale: education framing largely neutralizes the security penalty — it converts the project into Belsky's native language (Socratic tutoring = Study Mode philosophy, skills-to-employers = her Certifications launch, https://www.linkedin.com/posts/leahbelsky_excited-to-announce-oais-first-certifications-activity-7404173334101258241-G3Gc) and plausibly lifts her from 4 to ~7. But it trades away the contestant's strongest cards with the other four: the ground-truth-generation trick is clever but is a content-generation pattern ('compliant intern' output, Korevec's pejorative) rather than a verification pattern, the deep-Codex/300-tool technical ceiling is lower, and DFIR-as-subject-matter still requires four non-security judges to care about learning forensics — a niche audience Belsky herself would flag as a reach ceiling (her signature stat is 10M→140M learners). It optimizes for the one judge least representative of the panel's center of gravity.

security_coded_risk.concept: C — Evidence SDK (anti-hallucination citation middleware for any agent developer, Developer Tools)
security_coded_risk.effect: NEUTRAL on security-coding (it sheds the DFIR skin entirely) but HURTS on two other documented wires. Rationale: the universal 'trust layer for all agents' story is exactly the Rippletide-winning framing and would resonate conceptually with Sottiaux, Korevec, and Steinberger. However, middleware/framework packaging walks directly into Sottiaux's on-record aversions — 'scaffolding is coping not scaling' and 'we try to be very mindful when we introduce more opinionated frameworks... that could restrict flexibility' (SED transcript) — and Steinberger's explicit mockery: 'Almost all MCPs really should be CLIs' and anti-architecture-theater stance (https://steipete.me/posts/just-talk-to-it). An SDK also demos worst: no visceral live moment, weak on the Design quarter of the score (Sottiaux/Korevec both prize delight), and no named human whose day improves (Belsky's non-negotiable, lands ~3 with her). Judges cannot dogfood an SDK in a 3-minute review; they CAN watch an investigator catch a liar.

security_coded_risk.concept: Judging structure — do judges score all tracks or split (Belsky→Education etc.)?
security_coded_risk.effect: NEUTRAL/UNKNOWN — no analyst found evidence either way on https://openai.devpost.com/. Devpost norms and a 5-judge/4-track panel make a clean per-track split unlikely; the safer planning assumption is that all five judges see all finalists, meaning you cannot 'escape' Belsky by avoiding Education nor capture her by entering it. Consequence: picking track B primarily to court Belsky is a bet on an unverified mechanism; picking A and adding a 30-second Belsky-legible impact story (hours saved, expert amplified, access democratized) hedges both structures. This is the strategy's largest structural unknown — flagged honestly, not inferred.

reframing: Lead with the universal claim, not the domain: 'Every AI agent today is a confident intern with no footnotes. AIRTIGHT is an agent that proves its work — every sentence in its report is one click away from the exact file, byte offset, and command that backs it.' Forensics is introduced only as 'the hardest truth-critical proving ground there is,' never as the product category. This is the exact 'outputs to outcomes' framing that won Rippletide the Feb 2026 hackathon under two of these same judges.

Vocabulary swap — USE: prove, verify, receipts, evidence, trust, accountability, 'defensible artifact,' 'click-to-verify,' 'it caught its own mistake,' hours saved. AVOID: chain of custody, courtroom-grade, forensically sound, compliance, incident response, and all tool jargon (MFT, USN, Volatility, plaso, EWF) — Belsky and Korevec cannot evaluate it, Seshan flags it as insider jargon, and 'compliance' specifically trips Steinberger's anti-corporate-seriousness wire.

Never pitch fear. 'AI agents can't be trusted' sounds like Steinberger's own OpenClaw critics (he calls that discourse 'vitriol') and cuts against Belsky's AI-optimist brand. Flip it: 'here's how we made an agent trustworthy enough for the highest-stakes work.' Positive-sum trust, not FUD.

The 15-second demo beat, in order: (1) a non-forensicator types one plain-English sentence — 'someone deleted files on this laptop and tried to cover it up; find out what happened' (Steinberger's mum-test, Seshan's semi-technical superpowers); (2) the agent investigates autonomously for minutes while you narrate nothing (Sottiaux's long-running-agent taste); (3) a polished, beautiful report appears (Seshan's 'polished artifact at the end', the Design quarter); (4) click any claim → raw evidence with hash+offset+command; (5) the money shot: the adversarial verifier catches one of the agent's own findings failing cite-check and downgrades it ON CAMERA — Korevec's crit loop, Steinberger's hallucination anxiety, and Sottiaux's output-monitor thesis in one moment.

Neutralize the scaffolding critique preemptively: never say '300 tools.' Say 'the tools are just hands; the star is a delightfully simple loop — investigate, cite, adversarially verify, audit coverage.' Sottiaux preaches that harnesses constrain models; present the architecture as a for-loop with receipts and let model capability shine (SED transcript: 'it's a for loop and then a bunch of tool calls').

Give Belsky her 30 seconds: one named human, one number. 'A solo examiner's 300-hour backlog per case becomes 30 hours of review — and the expert keeps final authority over every finding.' That is structurally identical to the Estonia grading winner she personally spotlighted (378→100 hours, teacher review authority preserved). Add the access line: 'every small police department, school district, and nonprofit that can't afford a $400/hr examiner gets one.'

Universalize the impact ceiling for Sottiaux/Seshan: close with 'the citation-and-verification engine is domain-agnostic — this is the trust layer any agent needs before it can be delegated high-stakes work: audits, journalism, medical review, legal discovery.' You get Concept C's big-vision upside as a closing slide without shipping an SDK.

Anti-forensics demo framing: don't call it 'anti-forensics detection.' Call it 'the agent notices when someone lied to it' — a machine that detects tampering with its own evidence is instantly legible to all five judges and is inherently dramatic; the DFIR term buries the drama.

Design and dogfooding are scoring criteria, not garnish: ship real docs and an onboarding path (Korevec: docs 'not optional'), one moment of wit or personality in the product (Steinberger: 'I wanted it to be fun'; Korevec's Codex Pets), and tell the true dogfooding story — you are a DFIR practitioner who built the tool you depend on (Korevec: 'feeling the pain yourself builds urgency').
verdict.final_recommendation: Build AIRTIGHT (Concept A) in Work & Productivity, pitched as 'the agent that proves its work' with forensics as the proving ground — not as a security product. Against runner-up C (Evidence SDK): C keeps the same winning thesis but throws away everything that makes this contestant unbeatable on this panel. First, A demos viscerally and C doesn't — a live agent unmasking evidence-tampering and then catching its OWN unverifiable claim is theater every judge can feel in 15 seconds; middleware is a diagram, and 'slideware over software' is explicitly on Steinberger's alienation list. Second, C walks into the two most on-record aversions of the panel's two most technical judges: Sottiaux's 'scaffolding is coping / wary of opinionated frameworks' and Steinberger's 'almost all MCPs really should be CLIs' — an SDK IS the scaffolding pitch. Third, A maximizes the tiebreaker: Technological Implementation (Codex proficiency) breaks ties, and a long-running autonomous investigation over real disk images using the contestant's genuine 300-tool depth is a ceiling no consumer wrapper reaches — this is the criterion Sottiaux personally celebrates daily (100 Days of Codex) and Korevec is uniquely qualified to score. Fourth, A scores on Design (25%) through the click-to-verify dashboard, where C has almost no surface. Fifth, A subsumes C: the closing slide — 'the verification engine is domain-agnostic; this is the trust layer for all delegated agent work' — captures C's big-vision impact without C's demo weakness. Against B (CaseForge): B is a defensive crouch that optimizes for Belsky, the single judge least aligned with the panel's demonstrated center of gravity (verification systems), while abandoning the contestant's authentic expertise advantage; a 30-second hours-saved/access-democratized story inside A's pitch recovers most of Belsky's score (her own analysts put A at 6-6.5 with that framing) at zero cost. Predicted panel scores for well-framed A: roughly 8-9 (Sottiaux), 7.5-8 (Korevec), 7-8 (Seshan), 7+ (Steinberger), 6-6.5 (Belsky) — likely the strongest achievable hand across all three concepts. The verdict on the actual worry: deep-security is NOT the liability; security JARGON is. The panel just rewarded a verification layer at its last hackathon (Rippletide, Feb 2026, with two of these five judges on that jury) — the contestant is holding the winning thesis and worrying about the wrapper.
verdict.biggest_unknown: Whether all five judges score every track or the panel splits by track (e.g., Belsky judging only Education). No analyst found this on https://openai.devpost.com/ or elsewhere — and it materially changes the math: if judges split by track, Work & Productivity's actual sub-panel composition is unknown and the Belsky-mitigation effort may be moot (or, conversely, Education with only Belsky-types judging would strengthen Concept B's case). Secondary unknowns: judges' private tastes (X likes were unretrievable for Korevec, Steinberger, and Belsky — all analysts flagged this as a data gap, not evidence of disinterest), and whether any individual judge quote praising Rippletide's verification win exists (jury membership is verified; personal enthusiasm is inference). Recommended hedge: keep A's pitch fully legible to a track-agnostic panel, and check the Devpost rules page / hackathon Discord for the judging-assignment mechanics before the July 21 submission — it is the one fact that could shift the track choice.
